title: Part 3: Observability and Interpretability in Agentic AI
summary: This article surveys observability and interpretability techniques for agentic AI systems, addressing the challenge of understanding behavior that emerges from non-deterministic language models interacting with external tools and environments. It traces the evolution from feature-level interpretability (2015–2019) through reasoning chain interpretability (2020–2023) to trajectory-level interpretability (2023–present), arguing that agents require layered observability across execution paths. The piece covers OpenTelemetry standards for AI, evaluation methodologies (trace completeness, causal fidelity, latency overhead, actionability), and practical implementation guidance, positioning observability as critical for debugging failures and ensuring trustworthiness in production agentic systems.
content: Back to Articles Part 3: Observability and Interpretability in Agentic AI Community Article Published March 10, 2026 Upvote - Swastik Roy royswastik Follow Introduction From Model Interpretability to System Observability The Observability Stack for Agentic AI OpenTelemetry for AI Agents The Observability Architecture Tooling Landscape Evaluating Observability: What Makes a System Observable? Trace Completeness Causal Fidelity Latency Overhead Actionability Implementing Agent Observability: A Code Example Evaluating Reasoning Chain Quality Faithfulness Evaluation Coherence Evaluation Efficiency Evaluation Azure's Five Observability Best Practices Open Challenges References Introduction When an agentic AI system fails — producing an incorrect output, invoking the wrong tool, or entering an infinite loop — the first question is invariably: what happened? Unlike traditional software, where stack traces and log files provide deterministic records of execution, agentic AI systems present a fundamentally different observability challenge. Their behavior emerges from the interaction of a non-deterministic language model with external tools, memory systems, and dynamic environments, producing execution trajectories that are difficult to trace, reproduce, or explain. As Zhu et al. argue in their 2026 work on interpreting agentic systems, "errors introduced early in the reasoning chain may propagate" through subsequent steps, making it essential to observe not just the final output but the entire decision-making trajectory [1]. Raza et al. further distinguish between interpretability (access to internal reasoning processes), explainability (post-hoc accounts of behavior), and governance (organizational oversight mechanisms), noting that agentic AI requires all three [2]. This blog post surveys the research landscape on observability and interpretability for agentic AI systems, covering the theoretical foundations, established tooling, evaluation methodologies, and open challenges identified by the research community. From Model Interpretability to System Observability The interpretability literature has evolved through several distinct phases, each corresponding to the dominant AI architecture of its era. Zhu et al. trace this evolution as a "dual trajectory: an increase in" both system complexity and the sophistication of interpretability methods [1]. Feature-level interpretability (2015–2019) focused on understanding which input features influenced a model's prediction. Methods such as SHAP [3], LIME [4], and attention visualization provided insights into individual model decisions. These methods remain valuable for understanding the LLM component of an agentic system, but they cannot explain system-level behavior. Reasoning chain interpretability (2020–2023) emerged with chain-of-thought prompting [5] and the ReAct framework [6], which made the model's reasoning process partially observable through generated text. This represented a significant advance: for the first time, the model's "thought process" was externalized in a form that humans could inspect. Trajectory-level interpretability (2023–present) is the current frontier, driven by the recognition that agentic systems must be understood not through individual decisions but through their complete execution trajectories. As the ATLIS (Agentic Trajectory & Layered Interpretability Stack) framework proposes, observability must be "layered" across the system, from individual LLM calls through tool invocations to environment state changes [7]. The following table summarizes these three phases: Phase Era Unit of Analysis Key Methods Limitation for Agents Feature-level 2015–2019 Individual prediction SHAP, LIME, attention maps Cannot explain multi-step behavior Reasoning chain 2020–2023 Single reasoning trace CoT prompting, ReAct traces No tool/environment visibility Trajectory-level 2023–present Complete execution trajectory Trace-based logging, span analysis Scalability and standardization The Observability Stack for Agentic AI The research and engineering communities have converged on a layered observability architecture for agentic systems. This architecture draws from distributed systems observability (particularly the OpenTelemetry standard) and adapts it for the unique requirements of AI agents. OpenTelemetry for AI Agents OpenTelemetry (OTel), the industry standard for distributed systems observability, has been extended to support AI agent workloads. As the OpenTelemetry project's 2025 blog post on AI agent observability describes, "AI agent frameworks can report standardized metrics, traces, and logs, making it easier to integrate observability solutions and compare performance across" different systems [8]. The key abstraction is the span — a unit of work within a trace. In the context of agentic AI, spans correspond to distinct operations such as LLM inference calls, tool invocations, memory retrievals, and reasoning steps. AlSayyad et al. formalize this in their AgentTrace framework, which provides "structured logging" that "preserves trace and span" relationships across complex agent execution paths [9]. Siagian extends this approach specifically to agentic RAG systems, building an "OpenTelemetry-native agentic RAG benchmark harness that instruments routing, hybrid retrieval (BM25+FAISS), and answer generation with spans" [10]. This work demonstrates how standardized observability can enable systematic comparison of different agent architectures. The Observability Architecture The following diagram illustrates the layered observability architecture that has emerged from the research and tooling landscape: Diagram Tooling Landscape The observability tooling ecosystem for agentic AI has matured rapidly. The following table summarizes the major platforms and their capabilities: Tool Type Key Capabilities OpenTelemetry Support Notable Feature LangSmith [11] Commercial Tracing, evaluation, monitoring Yes (since Dec 2024) Deep LangChain/LangGraph integration Langfuse [12] Open-source Tracing, scoring, analytics Yes Framework-agnostic, self-hostable AgentOps [13] Open-source Session replay, cost tracking Partial Agent-specific event taxonomy Azure AI Foundry [14] Cloud platform Governance, evaluation, tracing Yes Enterprise-grade, integrated with Azure Arize Phoenix Open-source Trace visualization, evals Yes Strong embedding visualization Weights & Biases Commercial Experiment tracking, monitoring Partial ML experiment integration Evaluating Observability: What Makes a System Observable? Observability is not merely a tooling concern — it is an evaluable property of the system itself. Drawing from the distributed systems literature and recent AI-specific research, we can identify several dimensions along which observability should be assessed. Trace Completeness A fundamental requirement is that the observability system captures all significant operations in the agent's execution. This includes not just LLM calls and tool invocations but also internal reasoning steps, memory accesses, and decision points. AlSayyad et al. define trace completeness as the fraction of agent operations that are captured in the trace log [9]. Causal Fidelity The trace must accurately represent the causal relationships between operations. If a tool call was triggered by a specific reasoning step, the trace should capture this dependency. This is particularly challenging in systems with parallel execution or asynchronous tool calls. Latency Overhead Observability instrumentation must not significantly degrade the system's performance. The OpenTelemetry standard addresses this through sampling strategies and asynchronous export, but the overhead must be measured and bounded [8]. Actionability The most important dimension is whether the observability data enables meaningful action — debugging failures, identifying performance bottlenecks, and evaluating agent behavior. Azure's five observability best practices emphasize that observability should be "essential for delivering AI that is effective, transparent, safe, and aligned with organizational values" [14]. Implementing Agent Observability: A Code Example The following example demonstrates how to implement observability for an agentic system using the OpenTelemetry-compatible patterns established in the literature: """ Agent Observability Implementation Implements trace-based observability following the patterns described in AlSayyad et al. (2026) and the OpenTelemetry AI agent conventions. """ import time import json import uuid from dataclasses import dataclass, field from typing import List , Dict , Optional , Any from enum import Enum from contextlib import contextmanager class SpanKind ( Enum ): """Types of spans in an agent trace, following OTel conventions.""" AGENT_INVOCATION = "agent.invocation" LLM_INFERENCE = "llm.inference" TOOL_CALL = "tool.call" MEMORY_RETRIEVE = "memory.retrieve" MEMORY_STORE = "memory.store" REASONING_STEP = "agent.reasoning" DECISION_POINT = "agent.decision" ERROR = "agent.error" @dataclass class Span : """ A unit of work in an agent trace. Follows the OpenTelemetry span model adapted for AI agents. """ span_id: str trace_id: str parent_span_id: Optional [ str ] kind: SpanKind name: str start_time: float end_time: Optional [ float ] = None attributes: Dict [ str , Any ] = field(default_factory= dict ) events: List [ Dict [ str , Any ]] = field(default_factory= list ) status: str = "OK" @property def duration_ms ( self ) -> Optional [ float ]: if self.end_time: return (self.end_time - self.start_time) * 1000 return None @dataclass class Trace : """Complete trace of an agent execution.""" trace_id: str spans: List [Span] = field(default_factory= list ) metadata: Dict [ str , Any ] = field(default_factory= dict ) @property def root_span ( self ) -> Optional [Span]: for span in self.spans: if span.parent_span_id is None : return span return None @property def total_duration_ms ( self ) -> Optional [ float ]: root = self.root_span return root.duration_ms if root else None def get_children ( self, span_id: str ) -> List [Span]: return [s for s in self.spans if s.parent_span_id == span_id] class AgentTracer : """ Tracer for agentic AI systems. Provides context-managed span creation following the OpenTelemetry model, adapted for AI agent workloads as described in the OTel AI agent observability spec. """ def __init__ ( self ): self.traces: Dict [ str , Trace] = {} self._current_trace_id: Optional [ str ] = None self._span_stack: List [ str ] = [] def start_trace ( self, name: str = "agent_run" ) -> str : trace_id = str (uuid.uuid4())[: 16 ] self.traces[trace_id] = Trace( trace_id=trace_id, metadata={ "name" : name, "start_time" : time.time()} ) self._current_trace_id = trace_id return trace_id @contextmanager def span ( self, kind: SpanKind, name: str , **attributes ): """Context manager for creating and auto-closing spans.""" span = Span( span_id= str (uuid.uuid4())[: 12 ], trace_id=self._current_trace_id, parent_span_id=( self._span_stack[- 1 ] if self._span_stack else None ), kind=kind, name=name, start_time=time.time(), attributes=attributes ) trace = self.traces[self._current_trace_id] trace.spans.append(span) self._span_stack.append(span.span_id) try : yield span span.status = "OK" except Exception as e: span.status = "ERROR" span.events.append({ "name" : "exception" , "timestamp" : time.time(), "attributes" : { "exception.type" : type (e).__name__, "exception.message" : str (e) } }) raise finally : span.end_time = time.time() self._span_stack.pop() def get_trace ( self, trace_id: str ) -> Optional [Trace]: return self.traces.get(trace_id) class TraceAnalyzer : """ Analyzes agent traces for evaluation and debugging. Implements trajectory-level analysis as described in the ATLIS framework and AgentTrace. """ def analyze ( self, trace: Trace ) -> Dict [ str , Any ]: """Perform comprehensive trace analysis.""" spans = trace.spans return { "summary" : self._summarize(spans), "performance" : self._analyze_performance(spans), "tool_usage" : self._analyze_tool_usage(spans), "reasoning_quality" : self._analyze_reasoning(spans), "error_analysis" : self._analyze_errors(spans), "bottlenecks" : self._identify_bottlenecks(spans), } def _summarize ( self, spans: List [Span] ) -> Dict [ str , Any ]: """Generate trace summary statistics.""" kind_counts = {} for span in spans: kind = span.kind.value kind_counts[kind] = kind_counts.get(kind, 0 ) + 1 durations = [ s.duration_ms for s in spans if s.duration_ms is not None ] return { "total_spans" : len (spans), "span_types" : kind_counts, "total_duration_ms" : sum (durations) if durations else 0 , "error_count" : sum ( 1 for s in spans if s.status == "ERROR" ), } def _analyze_performance ( self, spans: List [Span] ) -> Dict [ str , Any ]: """Analyze performance characteristics.""" llm_spans = [ s for s in spans if s.kind == SpanKind.LLM_INFERENCE ] tool_spans = [ s for s in spans if s.kind == SpanKind.TOOL_CALL ] llm_durations = [ s.duration_ms for s in llm_spans if s.duration_ms is not None ] tool_durations = [ s.duration_ms for s in tool_spans if s.duration_ms is not None ] return { "llm_call_count" : len (llm_spans), "llm_total_ms" : sum (llm_durations) if llm_durations else 0 , "llm_avg_ms" : ( sum (llm_durations) / len (llm_durations) if llm_durations else 0 ), "tool_call_count" : len (tool_spans), "tool_total_ms" : ( sum (tool_durations) if tool_durations else 0 ), "total_tokens" : sum ( s.attributes.get( "llm.token_count" , 0 ) for s in llm_spans ), } def _analyze_tool_usage ( self, spans: List [Span] ) -> Dict [ str , Any ]: """Analyze tool usage patterns.""" tool_spans = [ s for s in spans if s.kind == SpanKind.TOOL_CALL ] tool_names = [ s.attributes.get( "tool.name" , "unknown" ) for s in tool_spans ] tool_success = sum ( 1 for s in tool_spans if s.status == "OK" ) return { "tools_used" : list ( set (tool_names)), "total_calls" : len (tool_spans), "success_rate" : ( tool_success / len (tool_spans) if tool_spans else 1.0 ), "tool_frequency" : { name: tool_names.count(name) for name in set (tool_names) }, } def _analyze_reasoning ( self, spans: List [Span] ) -> Dict [ str , Any ]: """Analyze reasoning chain quality.""" reasoning_spans = [ s for s in spans if s.kind == SpanKind.REASONING_STEP ] decision_spans = [ s for s in spans if s.kind == SpanKind.DECISION_POINT ] return { "reasoning_steps" : len (reasoning_spans), "decision_points" : len (decision_spans), "reasoning_to_action_ratio" : ( len (reasoning_spans) / max ( len (spans), 1 ) ), } def _analyze_errors ( self, spans: List [Span] ) -> Dict [ str , Any ]: """Analyze error patterns.""" error_spans = [s for s in spans if s.status == "ERROR" ] error_types = {} for span in error_spans: for event in span.events: if event.get( "name" ) == "exception" : etype = event[ "attributes" ].get( "exception.type" , "unknown" ) error_types[etype] = ( error_types.get(etype, 0 ) + 1 ) return { "total_errors" : len (error_spans), "error_types" : error_types, "error_rate" : len (error_spans) / max ( len (spans), 1 ), } def _identify_bottlenecks ( self, spans: List [Span] ) -> List [ Dict [ str , Any ]]: """Identify performance bottlenecks.""" bottlenecks = [] for span in spans: if span.duration_ms and span.duration_ms > 5000 : bottlenecks.append({ "span_id" : span.span_id, "kind" : span.kind.value, "name" : span.name, "duration_ms" : span.duration_ms, }) return sorted ( bottlenecks, key= lambda x: x[ "duration_ms" ], reverse= True ) # --- Example: Traced Agent Execution --- def example_traced_agent (): """Demonstrate a fully traced agent execution.""" tracer = AgentTracer() trace_id = tracer.start_trace( "question_answering" ) with tracer.span( SpanKind.AGENT_INVOCATION, "answer_question" , query= "What is the capital of France?" ): # Step 1: Reasoning with tracer.span( SpanKind.REASONING_STEP, "analyze_query" , reasoning= "This is a factual question about geography." ): time.sleep( 0.01 ) # Simulate processing # Step 2: Memory check with tracer.span( SpanKind.MEMORY_RETRIEVE, "check_memory" , query= "capital of France" ) as mem_span: time.sleep( 0.02 ) mem_span.attributes[ "memory.hit" ] = False # Step 3: Tool call with tracer.span( SpanKind.TOOL_CALL, "web_search" , **{ "tool.name" : "search" , "tool.input" : "capital of France" } ) as tool_span: time.sleep( 0.05 ) tool_span.attributes[ "tool.output" ] = ( "Paris is the capital of France" ) # Step 4: LLM inference with tracer.span( SpanKind.LLM_INFERENCE, "generate_response" , **{ "llm.model" : "gpt-4" , "llm.token_count" : 150 , "llm.prompt_tokens" : 100 , "llm.completion_tokens" : 50 } ): time.sleep( 0.03 ) # Step 5: Final response with tracer.span( SpanKind.DECISION_POINT, "select_response" , response= "The capital of France is Paris." ): time.sleep( 0.01 ) # Analyze the trace trace = tracer.get_trace(trace_id) analyzer = TraceAnalyzer() analysis = analyzer.analyze(trace) print ( "=== Trace Analysis ===" ) print (json.dumps(analysis, indent= 2 , default= str )) return trace, analysis if __name__ == "__main__" : example_traced_agent() Evaluating Reasoning Chain Quality Beyond operational observability, a critical evaluation dimension is the quality of the agent's reasoning process . The reasoning chain interpretability literature provides several established methods for this assessment. Faithfulness Evaluation A reasoning chain is faithful if it accurately reflects the causal process that led to the agent's decision. Lanham et al. demonstrated that chain-of-thought explanations are not always faithful — models sometimes arrive at correct answers through processes that differ from their stated reasoning [15]. For agentic systems, faithfulness evaluation is particularly important because unfaithful reasoning chains can mask dangerous decision-making patterns. Coherence Evaluation A reasoning chain is coherent if each step logically follows from the previous steps and the available information. Incoherent reasoning — where the agent contradicts itself, ignores relevant information, or makes unjustified logical leaps — is a common failure mode that observability systems should detect. Efficiency Evaluation The reasoning chain should be efficient — achieving the goal without unnecessary steps, redundant tool calls, or circular reasoning. This connects directly to the efficiency dimension of the evaluation taxonomy described in Part 1 of this series. The following diagram illustrates a reasoning chain evaluation pipeline: Diagram Azure's Five Observability Best Practices Microsoft Azure's Agent Factory team published five observability best practices for production AI agents that synthesize lessons from enterprise deployments [14]. These practices provide a useful framework for evaluating whether an observability implementation is adequate: Practice Description Evaluation Criterion Unified tracing Single trace across all agent components Can you follow a request end-to-end? Structured evaluation Automated quality checks on agent outputs Are outputs systematically scored? Cost and latency monitoring Track resource consumption per interaction Can you identify cost anomalies? Safety monitoring Real-time detection of unsafe behaviors Are safety violations flagged immediately? Feedback integration Connect user feedback to specific traces Can you correlate feedback with behavior? Open Challenges The research community has identified several persistent challenges in agentic AI observability: The observation-behavior gap. Observing an agent's actions (tool calls, outputs) is relatively straightforward. Observing its reasoning — why it chose one action over another — remains fundamentally difficult because the reasoning process is mediated by a neural network whose internal representations are not directly interpretable [1] [2]. Scalability of trace analysis. Production agent systems can generate enormous volumes of trace data. A single complex task might produce hundreds of spans across dozens of tool calls and LLM invocations. Developing scalable methods for analyzing this data — particularly for identifying patterns across many traces — remains an open engineering challenge [9]. Standardization. While OpenTelemetry provides a foundation, the semantic conventions for AI agent spans are still evolving. Different frameworks instrument different operations at different granularities, making cross-framework comparison difficult [8]. Privacy and security. Agent traces often contain sensitive information — user queries, tool outputs, internal reasoning about confidential data. Balancing the need for comprehensive observability with privacy and security requirements is a significant challenge, particularly in regulated industries [14]. References [1]: Zhu, J. et al. (2026). "Interpreting Agentic Systems: Beyond Model Explanations to System-Level Accountability." arXiv:2601.17168. [2]: Raza, S. et al. (2026). "Transparency in Agentic AI: A Survey of Interpretability, Explainability, and Governance." engrXiv. [3]: Lundberg, S. & Lee, S.-I. (2017). "A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions." NeurIPS 2017. [4]: Ribeiro, M.T. et al. (2016). "'Why Should I Trust You?': Explaining the Predictions of Any Classifier." KDD 2016. [5]: Wei, J. et al. (2022). "Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models." NeurIPS 2022. [6]: Yao, S. et al. (2022). "ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models." ICLR 2023. [7]: "Accountable Deployment of Agentic AI Demands Layered, System-Level Interpretability." (2026). Research Square preprint. [8]: OpenTelemetry (2025). "AI Agent Observability — Evolving Standards and Best Practices." https://opentelemetry.io/blog/2025/ai-agent-observability/ [9]: AlSayyad, A. et al. (2026). "AgentTrace: A Structured Logging Framework for Agent System Observability." LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems Workshop. [10]: Siagian, L. (2025). "End-to-End Observability for Agentic RAG: OpenTelemetry Traces with TruLens and LangSmith." ResearchGate. [11]: LangChain (2024). "Introducing OpenTelemetry Support for LangSmith." https://blog.langchain.com/opentelemetry-langsmith/ [12]: Langfuse (2026). "AI Agent Observability, Tracing & Evaluation with Langfuse." https://langfuse.com/blog/2024-07-ai-agent-observability-with-langfuse [13]: AgentOps (2024). "AgentOps: Enabling Observability of LLM Agents." arXiv:2411.05285. [14]: Microsoft Azure (2025). "Agent Factory: Top 5 Agent Observability Best Practices for Reliable AI." https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/blog/agent-factory-top-5-agent-observability-best-practices-for-reliable-ai/ [15]: Lanham, T. et al. (2023). "Measuring Faithfulness in Chain-of-Thought Reasoning." arXiv:2307.13702. takeaways: Agentic AI failures require trajectory-level observability, not just individual model decisions, because errors propagate through multi-step reasoning chains.
OpenTelemetry spans provide the standard abstraction for instrumenting AI agents across LLM calls, tool invocations, memory operations, and environment interactions.
Observable systems must be evaluated on trace completeness, causal fidelity (linking decisions to causes), latency overhead, and actionability for debugging.
Early errors in reasoning chains propagate downstream, making end-to-end trace visibility essential for root cause analysis in agent systems.

title: MemoHarness: Agent Harnesses That Learn from Experience
author: Huang, Yue, Wang, Wenjie, Bao, Han, Ma, Yuchen, Luo, Xiaonan, Nian, Yi, Zhuang, Haomin, Liu, Zheyuan, Zhao, Yue, Zhang, Xiangliang
summary: MemoHarness introduces an adaptive harness optimization framework that enables LLM-based agents to learn from their own executions without test-time labels or feedback. Rather than optimizing isolated components like prompts or pipelines, the work treats the agent harness—the external control layer managing context, tools, memory, and orchestration—as a learnable system. The framework decomposes the harness into six editable control dimensions and maintains a dual-layer experience bank storing per-case diagnoses and global patterns. When deployed on new tasks, MemoHarness retrieves relevant prior experience to adapt its configuration. Evaluations across shell agents, code generation, and reasoning benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements over fixed harnesses, with selective transfer to unseen tasks and models, while keeping computational overhead manageable through caching.
content: Computer Science > Artificial Intelligence arXiv:2607.14159 (cs) [Submitted on 14 Jul 2026] Title: MemoHarness: Agent Harnesses That Learn from Experience Authors: Yue Huang , Wenjie Wang , Han Bao , Yuchen Ma , Xiaonan Luo , Yi Nian , Haomin Zhuang , Zheyuan Liu , Yue Zhao , Xiangliang Zhang View a PDF of the paper titled MemoHarness: Agent Harnesses That Learn from Experience, by Yue Huang and 9 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: An agent harness is the external control layer that turns a base LLM into an executable agent by managing context, tools, orchestration, memory, decoding, and output handling. While harness design strongly affects agent behavior, most automatic improvement methods optimize narrower artifacts such as prompts, pipelines, or workflows, and deployed agents usually reuse a single global harness for all cases. We introduce MemoHarness, an adaptive harness optimization framework that learns from its own executions. MemoHarness decomposes the harness into six editable control dimensions, stores per-case diagnoses and distilled global patterns in a dual-layer experience bank, and adapts the learned harness to each test case using retrieved experience without test-time labels, feedback, or additional search. In our evaluation across shell-agent, code-generation, and analytical-reasoning benchmarks, MemoHarness improves over the fixed harnesses we compare against and shows selective transfer to unseen suites and base models. Its additional context can also remain cost-competitive when much of the retrieved experience is cacheable. These results provide evidence that execution experience is a practical substrate for building agent harnesses that are more adaptive than a single static configuration, while leaving broader claims about statistical robustness and component attribution to future work. takeaways: Agent harness design significantly impacts agent behavior but remains largely static; MemoHarness learns adaptive per-case configurations from execution traces.
Dual-layer experience bank stores both case-specific diagnostics and distilled global patterns, enabling efficient retrieval-based adaptation without retraining.
Framework decomposes harness into six editable control dimensions (context, tools, orchestration, memory, decoding, output), making optimization tractable.
Achieves improvements across diverse benchmarks (shell agents, code generation, reasoning) with selective transfer to unseen domains and base models.

title: Agentic Software: How AI Agents Are Restructuring the Software Paradigm
author: Cao, Zhenfeng
summary: This arXiv paper argues that AI agents powered by large language models represent a fundamental restructuring of software engineering, not merely an incremental tool improvement. The author distinguishes between traditional deterministic software, where code encodes pre-written logic, and agentic software, where the agent itself serves as the software with runtime-generated decision logic. The paper traces the historical evolution from licensed software through SaaS to Agent-as-a-Service (AaaS), each transferring increasing complexity from users. It introduces Agentic Engineering as a new discipline focused on agent systems rather than static code, with humans as intent architects rather than code authors. Using recent benchmarks (SWE-bench Verified, EvoClaw, LangChain studies), the paper demonstrates both the transformative potential and current limitations of this paradigm, concluding with a roadmap toward self-evolving agent ecosystems and practical recommendations for practitioners.
content: Computer Science > Software Engineering arXiv:2606.05608 (cs) [Submitted on 4 Jun 2026 ( v1 ), last revised 10 Jun 2026 (this version, v2)] Title: Agentic Software: How AI Agents Are Restructuring the Software Paradigm Authors: Zhenfeng Cao View a PDF of the paper titled Agentic Software: How AI Agents Are Restructuring the Software Paradigm, by Zhenfeng Cao View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: For over half a century, software engineering has operated on a foundational premise: human engineers decompose problems, encode decision logic into static code, and manually adapt that code as requirements evolve. This paper argues that the emergence of AI agents -- systems where large language models serve as the primary reasoning engine, dynamically generating and discarding code as an instrumental resource -- constitutes a fundamental restructuring of what software is, not an incremental tool improvement. We formalize the distinction between traditional deterministic software and agentic software: in the former, code is the carrier of pre-written decision logic; in the latter, the agent itself is the software, and its decision logic is generated at runtime. We trace the historical arc from licensed software to SaaS to Agent-as-a-Service (AaaS), showing that each shift transferred additional complexity away from end-users -- with the agentic shift transferring not just operational complexity but decision-making complexity itself. We introduce Agentic Engineering as an expansion of the software engineering discipline into a new paradigm, distinct in its core object of study (agent systems rather than static source code), its control model (LLM-driven rather than human-predefined), and its human role (intent architect rather than code author). Through analysis of recent benchmark evidence including SWE-bench Verified, EvoClaw, and LangChain's multi-agent coordination studies, we demonstrate both the transformative potential of the agentic paradigm and its current limitations. We conclude with a four-stage roadmap toward self-evolving agent ecosystems and concrete recommendations for practitioners navigating this transition. takeaways: AI agents fundamentally restructure software from static code to runtime-generated decision logic; code becomes an instrumental resource rather than the primary artifact.
Software engineering must evolve into Agentic Engineering with new disciplines: agent systems design, LLM-driven control, and intent architecture rather than code authorship.
Historical progression (Licensed → SaaS → AaaS) shows successive shifts of complexity away from end-users, culminating in agents handling decision-making itself.
Current agent benchmarks reveal both transformative potential and significant limitations; practitioners need concrete roadmaps and transition strategies.
Self-evolving agent ecosystems represent the four-stage roadmap destination, requiring rethinking of software development, deployment, and human roles.

title: Are Scaling Up Agent Environments And Evaluations
summary: This article examines the relationship between environment scale and agent evaluation methodologies. It argues that as AI agents become more capable and are deployed in increasingly complex environments, current evaluation practices need substantial rethinking. The key thesis is that scaling environments—adding complexity, state space size, and interactive richness—exposes fundamental limitations in how we measure agent performance. For technical readers, this matters because traditional metrics (success rate, reward accumulation) break down at scale, necessitating new evaluation frameworks that capture emergent behaviors, failure modes, and robustness properties. The article suggests that environment complexity and evaluation rigor must co-evolve.
takeaways: Current agent metrics fail to capture performance degradation in complex, high-dimensional environments at scale
Scaling environment complexity reveals new failure modes and emergent behaviors not visible in simple benchmarks
Evaluation frameworks must measure robustness, generalization, and graceful degradation alongside traditional success metrics
Environment design and evaluation methodology are interdependent—complexity changes what and how we should measure

title: https://community.openai.com/t/giving-tools-to-the-llm-vs-guiding-the-llm-and-using-tools/657475
summary: This community discussion explores the design trade-off between giving language models direct access to tools (function calling) versus guiding models through prompts while separately managing tool execution. The post examines whether LLMs should autonomously call functions or operate within human-controlled orchestration patterns. The distinction matters for reliability, interpretability, and control—direct tool access enables end-to-end automation but risks tool misuse, while guided approaches require more scaffolding but offer stronger guarantees about model behavior and output validity.
takeaways: Direct tool access enables autonomous LLM decision-making but increases risk of inappropriate or chained tool calls.
Guided orchestration (prompt-based routing + external tool management) trades flexibility for predictability and control.
Choice depends on use case: autonomous agents favor tool calling; safety-critical systems favor explicit orchestration.
Hybrid approaches can route simple queries autonomously while escalating complex decisions to human oversight.
Prompt engineering quality significantly impacts both patterns—poorly designed guidance undermines guided approaches.
